title: Dog Eye Proptosis: Emergency Guide for an Eye Out of Socket
meta_desc: If your dog's eye has popped out of its socket, this is a critical emergency. Learn the immediate steps to take and why urgent vet care is required.
content_html:
TL;DR: Proptosis is a critical veterinary emergency where a dog's eye bulges or pops out, requiring immediate medical attention to prevent permanent blindness. Keep the eye moist with sterile saline and transport your dog to an emergency clinic immediately without attempting to push the eye back in.

What is proptosis and why does it happen to dogs?

Proptosis is a serious medical condition where a dog's eyeball is displaced forward, causing it to bulge out or completely exit the bony eye socket. This is most common in brachycephalic (flat-faced) breeds like Pugs, Bulldogs, and Shih Tzus, whose shallow sockets make the eye more vulnerable to trauma, such as a fall, a fight with another animal, or accidental head pressure.

Is my dog's bulging eye a veterinary emergency and what should I do?

  • Recognize that this is a critical emergency where the optic nerve and blood vessels are being stretched, which can lead to permanent blindness within minutes.
  • Do not attempt to push the eye back into the socket yourself, as you may cause irreversible damage.
  • Keep the eye moist using sterile saline solution.
  • Transport your dog to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

How does a photo of my dog's eye help the emergency vet team?

  • Allows the triage staff to instantly assess the severity of the ocular displacement.
  • Helps the team identify signs of ruptured muscles or internal bleeding.
  • Enables the veterinary hospital to prepare surgical or stabilization equipment before you even enter the exam room.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Traumatic proptosis in dogs typically results from blunt trauma, leading to globe luxation from the orbit and eyelid spasms preventing retraction. Secondary orbital hemorrhage and swelling exacerbate globe displacement, resulting in corneal and conjunctival drying and malacia. Treatment necessitates immediate lubrication of the exposed cornea and conjunctiva, general anesthesia, lateral canthotomy, and temporary tarsorrhaphy. Systemic antibiotics and corticosteroids, combined with topical antibiotics and mydriatics, may be indicated. Prognosis is influenced by pupil size and reflexes, duration of exposure, other globe or orbital damage, and breed, with brachycephalic breeds being predisposed; approximately 40-50% of dogs regain vision following proptosis management.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1696)

faqs.question: Can my dog's vision be saved?
faqs.answer: Vision can sometimes be saved if the dog is treated immediately, though the prognosis depends on the severity of the trauma and how quickly the eye is surgically repositioned.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and prevent them from pawing at their face using an Elizabethan collar or by holding their paws. Keep the eye moist by gently placing a clean, water-soaked gauze or cloth over it.

faqs.question: Will my dog need surgery?
faqs.answer: In almost all cases of proptosis, surgery is required to either reposition the eye and suture the eyelids shut (tarsorrhaphy) or, if the damage is too severe, remove the eye entirely.
